text: Who gave you something recently that you're still using or thinking about?
context: A gift, a book, a recipe, a playlist. The thing has stuck around in your life and the giver probably doesn't know.
examples: I've been using that pan you gave me almost every week and I should have told you sooner.
That book you lent me is still on my nightstand. I keep going back to it.
The playlist you made me has been in heavy rotation. Thank you for the good taste.

text: Who spent real time on something for you lately, even if it looked small?
context: Time is the easiest thing to undercount. Someone gave you an hour, a careful read, a long walk, and it deserves naming.
examples: Thank you for the long call last week. I hung up feeling lighter than I had in days.
I know that edit took you longer than you let on, and it made the whole piece better.
You sat with me through that decision and didn't rush me. That mattered.

text: Whose recommendation turned out way better than you expected?
context: A show, a restaurant, a person to talk to. Tell them the bet paid off — recommenders rarely hear back.
examples: I finally watched the show you wouldn't shut up about and you were right, obviously.
That place you sent me to was perfect. Exactly the right night for it.
I took your advice on the meeting and it went well. Thanks for the nudge.

text: Who quietly held something together for you this month?
context: Someone kept the calendar moving, the kid fed, the project unblocked, without performing it. Easy to miss, worth saying out loud.
examples: I noticed you handled the whole back-and-forth with the school this week. Thank you for not making me ask.
You've been carrying more of the dinner stuff than I have lately and I see it.
Things didn't fall apart while I was heads-down, and that was you.

text: Who does something for the group that nobody actually thanks them for?
context: The friend who books the table, the colleague who runs notes, the parent who tracks birthdays. The labor is invisible until they stop.
examples: You're the reason this group still hangs out. Thank you for being the one who plans.
I realized you've been taking notes in every meeting and nobody's ever said anything. They're really good.
You remember everyone's birthday and it makes the family work. I notice.

text: Whose steady presence made a hard stretch less hard?
context: Not advice, not a fix — just someone who kept showing up. Name it directly; people often don't realize their presence counted.
examples: You didn't try to fix anything during that stretch and that was exactly what I needed.
Just knowing you were a text away made last month survivable.
Thank you for sitting with me when there wasn't anything to say.

text: Who taught you a habit you still use without thinking?
context: A way of taking notes, a way of cooking onions, a way of ending a meeting. The habit outlived the lesson — close the loop.
examples: I still take notes the way you showed me, twenty years later. It's just how I think now.
Every time I cook this I hear your voice telling me to be patient with the onions.
I learned how to actually end a meeting from watching you. I use it constantly.

text: Who shifted how you see something, and you never told them?
context: A throwaway line that rearranged a piece of your thinking. The speaker probably forgot the conversation; you didn't.
examples: Something you said years ago about choosing your hard things has stuck with me. I think about it a lot.
I haven't told you this, but the way you talked about your dad changed how I think about mine.
You said one thing in that car ride that I've been chewing on ever since.

text: Who modeled a kind of courage you ended up borrowing?
context: You watched them do a hard thing — quit, ask, leave, start — and it gave you cover to do your own version.
examples: Watching you leave that job is part of why I finally left mine. Thank you for going first.
You asked for what you wanted in front of me once and I've been quietly copying you ever since.
The way you handled saying no last year gave me permission to do the same.

text: Did a stranger do something small for you this week worth noting?
context: Held a door, let you merge, returned a dropped glove. You can't thank them, so just hold the moment for a second.
examples: The guy at the coffee place remembered my order and it made my whole morning.
Someone let me cut in line at the pharmacy and I'm still thinking about it.
A stranger picked up my scarf on the train and chased me down the platform.

text: When did someone give you their attention this week in a way that surprised you?
context: A barista who looked up, a neighbor who actually asked, a checkout person who slowed down. Brief, real, easy to miss.
examples: The cashier asked how my day was actually going and waited for the answer. Small thing, big thing.
My neighbor stopped me in the hall to ask about the move. I hadn't expected anyone to remember.
Someone at the gym noticed I was new and showed me where things were. That was nice of them.

text: Did you witness a small kindness between other people recently?
context: Not for you, but you saw it. A parent on the bus, two strangers on the corner, a colleague helping a new hire. Worth keeping.
examples: I watched a dad on the train be incredibly patient with his kid and it's stayed with me all day.
Two strangers helped someone pick up groceries on the corner and nobody made a thing of it.
I saw you walk the new person through the coffee machine yesterday. That was a kind thing to do.
